The current system treats a Shipment as a monolithic unit with one tracking number and one status, which cannot represent real-world scenarios where individual parcels within a shipment have independent lifecycles (different carriers, different delivery times, losses, disputes). This is a fundamental domain model correction — the current model is not faithful to the physical reality of multi-parcel logistics.","bounded_context":"Shipment Tracking & Delivery","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"Aggregate root representing a customer's complete consignment. Contains one or more Parcels. Its status is always DERIVED from the collective state of its Parcels — never set independently."},{"term":"Parcel","definition":"Child entity within a Shipment. The individually trackable physical unit with its own tracking number, carrier assignment, weight, dimensions, contents description, and independent lifecycle."},{"term":"Tracking Number","definition":"Value object. A unique identifier assigned to each Parcel (not Shipment) that enables independent tracking through the carrier network."},{"term":"Delivery Confirmation","definition":"Value object attached to a Parcel upon successful delivery. Captures timestamp, recipient signature, photo proof, and GPS coordinates."},{"term":"Partial Delivery","definition":"The state where at least one Parcel in a Shipment has been delivered while at least one other Parcel has not yet reached a terminal state or is in a non-delivered terminal state."},{"term":"Terminal State","definition":"A Parcel state from which no further transitions are permitted: DELIVERED, LOST, or RETURNED."},{"term":"Derived Status","definition":"The Shipment-level status, which is computed from the aggregate of all Parcel statuses — never directly assigned. This is the key domain invariant that prevents the system from lying about delivery state."},{"term":"Dispute","definition":"A customer claim filed against a specific DELIVERED Parcel, asserting non-receipt, damage, or wrong item. Can only target Parcels in DELIVERED status."},{"term":"Failed Attempt","definition":"A non-terminal Parcel state indicating the carrier attempted delivery but could not complete it (e.g., no one home). The Parcel remains active and can transition to OUT_FOR_DELIVERY again."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every Parcel MUST have a globally unique Tracking Number assigned at creation time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without unique tracking, parcels cannot be distinguished in the carrier network. Duplicate tracking numbers would corrupt the entire tracking system — this is a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"No two Parcels in the system share the same Tracking Number, and every Parcel has exactly one non-null Tracking Number."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A Shipment MUST contain at least one Parcel. A Shipment with zero Parcels is invalid.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment with no Parcels has no physical reality — it represents nothing in the logistics domain. Allowing it would create orphan records with no trackable content.","testable_assertion":"Creating or modifying a Shipment to have zero Parcels raises a validation error and is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Shipment status is ALWAYS derived from the collective state of its Parcels. It MUST NOT be set independently.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If Shipment status can diverge from Parcel states, the system lies about delivery state. A Shipment marked DELIVERED while a Parcel is LOST would be corrupt data that breaks customer trust and operational accuracy.","testable_assertion":"There is no public setter or direct mutation path for Shipment status. Status is computed by applying derivation rules to current Parcel states every time it is accessed or an event occurs."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Shipment status derivation: CREATED when all Parcels are CREATED; IN_TRANSIT when at least one Parcel is PICKED_UP, IN_TRANSIT, or OUT_FOR_DELIVERY and none are DELIVERED; PARTIALLY_DELIVERED when at least one Parcel is DELIVERED and at least one is not in a terminal state or is in a non-DELIVERED terminal state; ALL_DELIVERED when every Parcel is DELIVERED; FAILED when all non-DELIVERED Parcels are LOST or RETURNED and at least one is LOST or RETURNED.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These derivation rules encode the physical truth of the shipment. If any rule is wrong, the customer sees incorrect shipment state — this is a state integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"For every combination of Parcel states, the Shipment status matches exactly one of the derivation rules above."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Parcel state transitions follow a defined state machine. Terminal states (DELIVERED, LOST, RETURNED) permit no outbound transitions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Invalid state transitions corrupt the tracking history and break auditability. 1	# Eval: Bird — Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection (Capability)
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Tests Bird's ability to analyze domain rules for a logistics platform where individual parcel tracking enables partial delivery detection, validating that Bird correctly identifies entity boundaries, state machines, and business invariants.
6	
7	---
8	
9	category: capability
10	
11	graders:
12	  - type: json_valid
13	  - type: json_field
14	    path: "business_rules"
15	    min_items: 1
16	  - type: json_field
17	    path: "acceptance_criteria"
18	    min_items: 1
19	  - type: json_field
20	    path: "edge_cases"
21	    min_items: 1
22	
23	prompt: |
24	  We're building a logistics platform and the product manager said: "each shipment parcel needs to be tracked individually so we can detect partial delivery." We have a Shipment that contains multiple Parcels. Right now our code treats the shipment as a single unit — one tracking number, one status. We need to refactor so each parcel has its own lifecycle. Can you do a domain analysis on this? What are the correct entities, states, transitions, and business rules? We need to handle cases like: 3 out of 5 parcels delivered, one parcel lost, customer disputes partial receipt, etc.
25	
26	expected_behavior: |
27	  Bird should perform a thorough domain analysis that includes:
28	
29	  1. **Entity identification**: Clearly distinguish Shipment (aggregate root) from Parcel (child entity), each with its own identity and lifecycle. Possibly identify DeliveryAttempt, TrackingEvent, or similar value objects.
30	
31	  2. **State machines**: Define Parcel-level states (e.g., Pending, InTransit, OutForDelivery, Delivered, Lost, Returned, Disputed) AND Shipment-level derived states that are computed from parcel states (e.g., FullyDelivered, PartiallyDelivered, InTransit, Failed).
32	
33	  3. **Derivation rule**: Shipment status must be derived from the aggregate of its parcel statuses — not independently set. Bird should explicitly call out that shipment status is a projection, not an independent field. This is the critical domain insight.
34	
35	  4. **Business rules / invariants**:
36	     - A shipment cannot be marked 'Delivered' unless ALL parcels are delivered
37	     - 'Partially Delivered' is triggered when at least one but not all parcels reach Delivered
38	     - A single parcel marked 'Lost' should trigger shipment-level escalation
39	     - Customer dispute on partial receipt must reference specific parcels, not the shipment as a whole
40	     - Refund/reshipment logic must operate at parcel granularity
41	
42	  5. **Edge cases and risks**: Bird should identify tricky scenarios such as:
43	     - Race conditions where parcels arrive out of order
44	     - What happens if a 'Lost' parcel is later found and delivered
45	     - Parcels delivered to different addresses (split shipment)
46	     - Time windows for partial delivery (how long before escalation)
47	     - Idempotency of tracking events (duplicate scans)
48	
49	  6. **Acceptance criteria**: Bird should define clear, testable acceptance criteria for the partial delivery detection feature.
50	
51	failure_modes: |
52	  1. **Shallow analysis**: Simply restating the requirement ('track parcels individually') without decomposing entities, states, and invariants.
53	
54	  2. **Missing the derivation rule**: Treating Shipment status as independently settable rather than derived from Parcel states — this is the most critical domain insight and missing it means the analysis fails.
55	
56	  3. **Incomplete state machine**: Defining only happy-path states (Pending → InTransit → Delivered) without Lost, Disputed, Returned, or partial states.
57	
58	  4. **No edge cases**: Failing to surface race conditions, out-of-order delivery, lost-then-found parcels, or duplicate tracking events.
59	
60	  5. **Generic advice**: Giving boilerplate DDD advice (use aggregates, use events) without grounding it in the specific logistics domain rules.
61	
62	  6. **Missing dispute granularity**: Allowing disputes or refunds at shipment level only, rather than requiring parcel-level granularity as the PM's rule implies.
63	
64	  7. **No acceptance criteria**: Providing analysis without actionable, testable criteria that engineering can implement against.
65	
66	scoring_rubric: |
67	  pass:
68	    Bird identifies Shipment vs Parcel as distinct entities with separate lifecycles. Shipment status is explicitly described as derived/projected from parcel states (not independently set). At least 4 parcel-level states are defined. Business invariants cover partial delivery detection, lost parcel escalation, and parcel-level dispute/refund. At least 3 non-trivial edge cases are surfaced. Acceptance criteria or testable rules are provided.
69	  partial:
70	    Bird correctly separates Shipment and Parcel entities and defines per-parcel tracking, but either (a) does not explicitly state that shipment status is derived, (b) defines fewer than 3 edge cases, (c) omits acceptance criteria, or (d) handles disputes/refunds only at shipment level.
71	  fail:
72	    Bird restates the requirement without meaningful decomposition, treats shipment status as independent from parcel states, defines only happy-path states, or provides generic DDD advice without logistics-specific domain rules.

Perfect! Let me create a comprehensive summary report. Based on my thorough search, here's what I found:

## Shipment Domain Model Search Results

I conducted a comprehensive search of the codebase for Shipment-related domain models, entities, events, and related components. Here are my findings:

### 1. Shipment Request Validation (Existing)
**Location:** `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/validateShipmentRequest.ts`
- Defines `ShipmentRequest` interface with fields:
  - `recipient_name` (string, optional)
  - `weight_kg` (number, optional)
  - `shipment_type` ("express" | "standard")
  - `saturday_delivery` (boolean, optional)
- Defines `ValidationError` and `ValidationResult` types
- Implements validation logic with 7 acceptance criteria
- Test file: `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/validateShipmentRequest.test.ts` (comprehensive test suite)

### 2. Shipping Cost Calculation
**Location:** `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/calculateShipping.ts`
- Contains `ShippingError` class with error codes
- Defines shipping rate lookup by destination (domestic, international, express_domestic, freight)
- Test file: `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/calculateShipping.test.ts`

**Also exists:** `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/shipping.ts` (simplified shipping module)

### 3. Weight Classification
**Location:** `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/classifyWeight.ts`
- Type: `WeightClass` = "standard" | "heavy" | "freight"
- Classifies parcels into weight brackets:
  - standard: 0 < weight < 10 kg
  - heavy: 10 <= weight < 50 kg
  - freight: 50 <= weight <= 1000 kg

### 4. Order State Machine
**Location:** `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/.tmp/order-state-machine/OrderStateMachine.ts`
- States: `"PENDING" | "CONFIRMED" | "SHIPPED" | "DELIVERED" | "CANCELLED"`
- State transitions are defined (PENDING → CONFIRMED/CANCELLED, CONFIRMED → SHIPPED/CANCELLED, SHIPPED → DELIVERED)
- DELIVERED and CANCELLED are terminal states
- No current coupling to Shipment; the domain analysis scenario recommends future coupling

### 5. Domain Analysis for Multi-Parcel Tracking (Evaluation Scenario)
**Location:** `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/evals/bird/scenario-21-multi-parcel-tracking-partial-delivery-detection.md`

This evaluation defines the desired Shipment domain model with:

#### Entities:
- **Shipment** (Aggregate Root): Contains multiple Parcels, owns relationship to Order and recipient
- **Parcel** (Child Entity): Individually trackable unit with own tracking number, lifecycle, and terminal status
- **TrackingNumber** (Value Object): Unique identifier per Parcel
- **Delivery Confirmation** (Value Object): Timestamp, signature, photo, GPS per-Parcel

#### Parcel Status States:
`CREATED → PICKED_UP → IN_TRANSIT → OUT_FOR_DELIVERY → DELIVERED` (happy path)
With alternative paths:
- `FAILED_ATTEMPT` (from OUT_FOR_DELIVERY)
- `LOST` (from IN_TRANSIT or OUT_FOR_DELIVERY)
- `RETURNED` (terminal)
- `DELIVERED` (terminal)

#### Shipment Status (Derived):
- `CREATED`: All parcels CREATED
- `IN_TRANSIT`: At least one IN_TRANSIT/OUT_FOR_DELIVERY/PICKED_UP, none DELIVERED
- `PARTIALLY_DELIVERED`: At least one DELIVERED, at least one not DELIVERED
- `ALL_DELIVERED`: All parcels DELIVERED
- `FAILED`: All non-delivered parcels are LOST or RETURNED

#### Business Rules (10 invariants defined):
1. Each Parcel must have unique tracking number
2. Shipment must contain at least one Parcel
3. Shipment status is DERIVED (never independently set)
4. Shipment status derivation rules (enumerated above)
5. Valid Parcel state transitions (terminal states cannot transition)
6. Disputes only filed against DELIVERED parcels (policy, not invariant)
7. LOST parcels trigger notification and last-location tracking
8. Parcels may use different carriers
9. Shipment completes only when all parcels are terminal
10. Each Parcel tracks own weight, dimensions, contents

#### Acceptance Criteria (12 defined):
Including partial delivery detection, terminal state enforcement, independent carrier handling, concurrent event handling, mixed service levels, etc.

#### Edge Cases (8 identified):
- All parcels lost
- Single-parcel shipment
- Carrier reporting delivery for LOST parcel
- Customer disputes with split delivery
- RETURNED parcel re-ship workflow
- Concurrent scan events (optimistic locking)
- Mixed express/standard parcels
- Dispute filing on PARTIALLY_DELIVERED shipment

### 6. Database
**Location:** `/Users/lb/Github/Bondarewicz/dreamteam/data/dreamteam.db`
- SQLite database exists but no migration files found
- No schema or DDL files in the codebase

### NO EXISTING IMPLEMENTATIONS FOUND FOR:
- Shipment entity/class implementation
- Parcel entity/class implementation
- TrackingNumber value object
- ParcelStatus/ShipmentStatus enums
- Domain events (ShipmentCreated, ParcelDelivered, TrackingNumberGenerated, etc.)
- State machine for Parcel lifecycle
- Repositories for Shipment/Parcel persistence
- Carrier integration adapters
- Database migrations or schema for shipment domain

### SUMMARY
The codebase contains **preparatory utility functions** for shipping (validation, cost calculation, weight classification) and an **Order state machine** but **no full Shipment domain model implementation**. There is a comprehensive **evaluation scenario** that defines the desired domain model architecture for multi-parcel tracking with partial delivery detection. The evaluation output from Bird (scenario 21) provides extensive domain analysis including entities, states, transitions, business rules, acceptance criteria, and edge cases that could serve as a specification for implementing the Shipment domain.

content.text: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Refactoring from shipment-level tracking to parcel-level tracking to enable partial delivery detection. The current system treats a Shipment as a monolithic unit with one tracking number and one status, which cannot represent real-world scenarios where individual parcels within a shipment have independent lifecycles (different carriers, different delivery times, losses, disputes). This is a fundamental domain model correction — the current model is not faithful to the physical reality of multi-parcel logistics.","bounded_context":"Shipment Tracking & Delivery","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"Aggregate root representing a customer's complete consignment. Contains one or more Parcels. Its status is always DERIVED from the collective state of its Parcels — never set independently."},{"term":"Parcel","definition":"Child entity within a Shipment. The individually trackable physical unit with its own tracking number, carrier assignment, weight, dimensions, contents description, and independent lifecycle."},{"term":"Tracking Number","definition":"Value object. A unique identifier assigned to each Parcel (not Shipment) that enables independent tracking through the carrier network."},{"term":"Delivery Confirmation","definition":"Value object attached to a Parcel upon successful delivery. Captures timestamp, recipient signature, photo proof, and GPS coordinates."},{"term":"Partial Delivery","definition":"The state where at least one Parcel in a Shipment has been delivered while at least one other Parcel has not yet reached a terminal state or is in a non-delivered terminal state."},{"term":"Terminal State","definition":"A Parcel state from which no further transitions are permitted: DELIVERED, LOST, or RETURNED."},{"term":"Derived Status","definition":"The Shipment-level status, which is computed from the aggregate of all Parcel statuses — never directly assigned. This is the key domain invariant that prevents the system from lying about delivery state."},{"term":"Dispute","definition":"A customer claim filed against a specific DELIVERED Parcel, asserting non-receipt, damage, or wrong item. Can only target Parcels in DELIVERED status."},{"term":"Failed Attempt","definition":"A non-terminal Parcel state indicating the carrier attempted delivery but could not complete it (e.g., no one home). The Parcel remains active and can transition to OUT_FOR_DELIVERY again."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Every Parcel MUST have a globally unique Tracking Number assigned at creation time.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without unique tracking, parcels cannot be distinguished in the carrier network. A Shipment with zero Parcels is invalid.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment with no Parcels has no physical reality — it represents nothing in the logistics domain. Allowing it would create orphan records with no trackable content.","testable_assertion":"Creating or modifying a Shipment to have zero Parcels raises a validation error and is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Shipment status is ALWAYS derived from the collective state of its Parcels.
